▸
View problem statement
Problem statement
Design the dispatch core of a ride-sharing service. Riders request a ride from a pickup to a drop location; the system matches each request to a nearby available driver and tracks the trip lifecycle. Many riders may request at once and compete for the same drivers.

Core Requirements:

Request Ride: a rider requests a ride; the system picks a suitable available driver and assigns the ride.
Matching Policy: which driver to pick should be pluggable (nearest, highest-rated).
Trip Lifecycle: a ride moves REQUESTED -> ASSIGNED -> ONGOING -> COMPLETED (or CANCELLED); a driver moves AVAILABLE -> ASSIGNED -> ON_TRIP -> AVAILABLE.
Driver Availability: drivers go online/offline; only AVAILABLE drivers can be matched.
Error Handling: reject when no driver is available, invalid locations, unknown rides/drivers, and illegal lifecycle transitions.

Additional Considerations:

No driver is assigned to two riders at once.
Thread-safety when many riders race for the same scarce drivers; prefer an optimistic pick with a pessimistic confirm.

Design Considerations:

Use Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) principles.
Identify key classes (e.g. DispatchService, Driver, Rider, Ride, Location, DriverMatchStrategy).
Model the ride and driver as state machines.
Use an optimistic candidate pick then confirm under a per-driver lock, retrying the next candidate if lost.
Discuss design patterns (Strategy, State, Template Method for the lock hook).
Focus on extensibility for new matching policies and thread-safety.

# Python 3.11+
"""Domain model for the Ride Sharing / Driver Dispatch system.

The "nouns" live here: the `Location` value object, the two state-machine
entities (`Driver` and `Ride`), the lightweight `Rider`, the enums that name
every state, and one typed-exception family rooted at `RideError`.

🎙️ "I keep all the state-transition rules ON the entities — a Driver and a Ride
each guard their own state machine. The orchestrator coordinates; it never
reaches in and sets a status field directly. That makes illegal jumps impossible
by construction, not by convention."
"""

import math
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


# ── VOCABULARY (enums name every legal state) ────────────────────────────────
class DriverState(Enum):
    OFFLINE = "OFFLINE"      # not accepting rides (just logged in, or stepped away)
    AVAILABLE = "AVAILABLE"  # online and matchable — the ONLY state a match can pick
    ASSIGNED = "ASSIGNED"    # promised to a ride, heading to the pickup
    ON_TRIP = "ON_TRIP"      # carrying the rider from pickup to drop


class RideState(Enum):
    REQUESTED = "REQUESTED"  # rider asked; no driver yet
    ASSIGNED = "ASSIGNED"    # a driver is locked in for this ride
    ONGOING = "ONGOING"      # the trip is in progress
    COMPLETED = "COMPLETED"  # delivered to the drop — terminal
    CANCELLED = "CANCELLED"  # could not be served / called off — terminal


# ── EXCEPTIONS (one base, one subclass per failure mode) ─────────────────────
class RideError(Exception):
    """Base for every error this system raises — callers catch one type."""


class NoDriverAvailableError(RideError):
    """No AVAILABLE driver could be confirmed for the request."""


class InvalidLocationError(RideError):
    """A pickup or drop location is outside the valid lat/lng range."""


class UnknownRiderError(RideError):
    """request_ride() was given a rider id the system does not know."""


class UnknownDriverError(RideError):
    """A driver id was given that the system does not know."""


class UnknownRideError(RideError):
    """A ride id was given that the system does not know."""


class IllegalRideTransitionError(RideError):
    """A ride was asked to make a state jump its lifecycle forbids."""


class IllegalDriverTransitionError(RideError):
    """A driver was asked to make a state jump its lifecycle forbids."""


# ── VALUE OBJECT: Location ───────────────────────────────────────────────────
@dataclass(frozen=True)
class Location:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A point on the map. Frozen because a coordinate never "changes" — a rider
    # who moves gets a NEW Location, we never mutate an old one. Owning
    # `distance_to` here is Information Expert: the thing that holds the coords
    # is the thing that should know how far apart two of them are.
    lat: float
    lng: float

    def distance_to(self, other: "Location") -> float:
        # WHY: flat-plane (Euclidean) distance. This is an APPROXIMATION — it
        #      ignores the curvature of the Earth. That is fine here because we
        #      only use it to RANK nearby drivers, and over a few km the ordering
        #      a flat-plane metric gives matches haversine. For real distances
        #      (fares, ETAs) we'd swap in haversine; the call site never changes.
        return math.hypot(self.lat - other.lat, self.lng - other.lng)

    def is_valid(self) -> bool:
        # WHY: a Location knows its own validity rule. The service calls this at
        #      the public boundary so a bad coordinate is rejected before any
        #      state changes.
        return -90.0 <= self.lat <= 90.0 and -180.0 <= self.lng <= 180.0


# ── ENTITY: Rider (a lightweight, looked-up entity) ──────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Rider:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The system stores riders so request_ride can look one up by id and reject
    # an unknown caller. It holds no lifecycle of its own — its location is just
    # where it currently is. Kept as its own class (not an actor) only because
    # the orchestrator needs a registry to validate the rider id against.
    id: str
    location: Location


# ── ENTITY: Driver (a guarded state machine) ─────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Driver:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A driver is a state machine: OFFLINE → AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP →
    # AVAILABLE. Only THIS class may change `state`, and every transition is
    # guarded — an illegal jump raises instead of silently corrupting state.
    # This is the resource many riders compete for.
    id: str
    location: Location
    rating: float
    state: DriverState = DriverState.OFFLINE

    def go_online(self) -> None:
        # WHY: going online from AVAILABLE again is a harmless no-op — safe to
        #      call twice (idempotent). You can only come online from OFFLINE;
        #      coming "online" mid-trip is nonsense, so it raises.
        if self.state == DriverState.AVAILABLE:
            return
        if self.state != DriverState.OFFLINE:
            raise IllegalDriverTransitionError(
                f"driver {self.id} cannot go online from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = DriverState.AVAILABLE

    def go_offline(self) -> None:
        # WHY: a driver may step away only when idle. Going offline while OFFLINE
        #      is a no-op (idempotent); going offline mid-trip would strand a
        #      rider, so ASSIGNED/ON_TRIP raises.
        if self.state == DriverState.OFFLINE:
            return
        if self.state != DriverState.AVAILABLE:
            raise IllegalDriverTransitionError(
                f"driver {self.id} cannot go offline from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = DriverState.OFFLINE

    def assign(self) -> None:
        # WHY: this is the line the per-driver lock protects. Only an AVAILABLE
        #      driver can be claimed; the very next claimer sees ASSIGNED and is
        #      turned away — that is what stops two riders sharing one driver.
        if self.state != DriverState.AVAILABLE:
            raise IllegalDriverTransitionError(
                f"driver {self.id} cannot be assigned from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = DriverState.ASSIGNED

    def start_trip(self) -> None:
        if self.state != DriverState.ASSIGNED:
            raise IllegalDriverTransitionError(
                f"driver {self.id} cannot start a trip from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = DriverState.ON_TRIP

    def complete(self) -> None:
        # WHY: finishing a trip frees the driver back to AVAILABLE so they can be
        #      matched again — the resource returns to the pool.
        if self.state != DriverState.ON_TRIP:
            raise IllegalDriverTransitionError(
                f"driver {self.id} cannot complete from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = DriverState.AVAILABLE


# ── ENTITY: Ride (a guarded state machine) ───────────────────────────────────
@dataclass
class Ride:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # A ride is the OTHER state machine: REQUESTED → ASSIGNED → ONGOING →
    # COMPLETED, or → CANCELLED if no driver can be found. A ride belongs to
    # exactly one request, so (unlike a Driver) it is never contended — the
    # request that created it is its only writer.
    id: str
    rider: Rider
    pickup: Location
    drop: Location
    driver: Driver | None = None
    state: RideState = RideState.REQUESTED

    def assign(self, driver: Driver) -> None:
        # WHY: a ride can only be assigned once, straight out of REQUESTED. The
        #      driver reference is recorded here so the trip lifecycle can drive
        #      the driver's state machine in lock-step.
        if self.state != RideState.REQUESTED:
            raise IllegalRideTransitionError(
                f"ride {self.id} cannot be assigned from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.driver = driver
        self.state = RideState.ASSIGNED

    def start(self) -> None:
        if self.state != RideState.ASSIGNED:
            raise IllegalRideTransitionError(
                f"ride {self.id} cannot start from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = RideState.ONGOING

    def complete(self) -> None:
        if self.state != RideState.ONGOING:
            raise IllegalRideTransitionError(
                f"ride {self.id} cannot complete from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = RideState.COMPLETED

    def cancel(self) -> None:
        # WHY: a request that finds no driver is recorded as CANCELLED rather
        #      than vanishing — every request reaches a defined outcome we can
        #      audit later. Only a not-yet-started ride may be cancelled.
        if self.state not in (RideState.REQUESTED, RideState.ASSIGNED):
            raise IllegalRideTransitionError(
                f"ride {self.id} cannot be cancelled from {self.state.name}"
            )
        self.state = RideState.CANCELLED

# Python 3.11+
"""Driver-matching policies — the seam that decides WHICH driver to prefer.

🎙️ "The dispatch service does not hardcode 'nearest wins'. It asks a
DriverMatchStrategy to RANK the available drivers, then confirms them one by one.
Crucially the strategy is read-only — it returns an ordered candidate list and
mutates nothing. That separation is what lets me pick optimistically WITHOUT a
lock and confirm pessimistically WITH one. Switching policy is a new class, zero
service changes."
"""

from abc import ABC, abstractmethod

from models import Driver, Location


class DriverMatchStrategy(ABC):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The abstraction DispatchService depends on (Dependency Inversion). It only
    # RANKS — it never claims a driver. Returning a list (not a single driver)
    # is the whole trick: the service can fall through to the next candidate when
    # its first pick was snatched by another rider, with no help from the policy.
    @abstractmethod
    def rank(self, drivers: list[Driver], pickup: Location) -> list[Driver]:
        """Return the AVAILABLE drivers in preference order. Must not mutate."""
        raise NotImplementedError


class NearestDriverStrategy(DriverMatchStrategy):
    """Prefer the driver physically closest to the pickup point."""

    def rank(self, drivers: list[Driver], pickup: Location) -> list[Driver]:
        # WHY: a fresh sorted list — we never reorder the caller's list in place,
        #      so the service's optimistic read stays a clean snapshot.
        return sorted(drivers, key=lambda d: d.location.distance_to(pickup))


class HighestRatedStrategy(DriverMatchStrategy):
    """Prefer the highest-rated driver; tie-break by closeness to the pickup."""

    def rank(self, drivers: list[Driver], pickup: Location) -> list[Driver]:
        # WHY: sort by rating DESC, then distance ASC as a stable tie-break, so
        #      two 4.9-star drivers still come back nearest-first.
        return sorted(
            drivers,
            key=lambda d: (-d.rating, d.location.distance_to(pickup)),
        )

# Python 3.11+
"""DispatchService — the orchestrator — plus its thread-safe subclass.

`DispatchService` is the clean, single-threaded-correct core. It owns the
registries (drivers, riders, rides) and runs the optimistic-pick /
pessimistic-confirm matching loop. It contains ZERO real locks: every place that
mutates a driver's state is wrapped in a `_lock(driver)` hook that does nothing
here.

`ThreadSafeDispatchService` is the second version. It overrides that single hook
with a per-driver RLock (guarded by a map lock) and changes nothing else — the
clean → thread-safe diff is one method.
"""

import itertools
import threading
from collections.abc import Iterator
from contextlib import contextmanager

from models import (
    Driver,
    DriverState,
    InvalidLocationError,
    Location,
    NoDriverAvailableError,
    Ride,
    Rider,
    RideState,
    UnknownDriverError,
    UnknownRideError,
    UnknownRiderError,
)
from strategies import DriverMatchStrategy


class DispatchService:
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The brain. Every public call flows through here. It owns the three
    # registries (drivers, riders, rides) and coordinates the two state machines
    # — but it NEVER sets a status field directly; it asks the entity to
    # transition itself. Matching is delegated to a strategy, so the service
    # has no idea whether "nearest" or "highest-rated" is wired in.
    def __init__(
        self,
        strategy: DriverMatchStrategy,
        drivers: list[Driver] | None = None,
        riders: list[Rider] | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        self._strategy = strategy
        self._drivers: dict[str, Driver] = {d.id: d for d in (drivers or [])}
        self._riders: dict[str, Rider] = {r.id: r for r in (riders or [])}
        self._rides: dict[str, Ride] = {}
        # WHY: a counter, not UUIDs, so ride ids read "ride-1", "ride-2" in tests
        #      and demos. In production I'd swap in UUIDs — the call site is one
        #      helper, so the switch is trivial.
        self._ids = itertools.count(1)

    # ── the lock hook (Template Method) ──
    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, driver: Driver) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: this does NOTHING in the clean core — there is not a single real
        #      lock in this file. The thread-safe subclass overrides ONLY this
        #      method with a real per-driver lock. So the whole concurrency story
        #      is one method, and the readable design stays the star.
        yield

    # ── public API ──
    def request_ride(self, rider_id: str, pickup: Location, drop: Location) -> Ride:
        # role: the matching pipeline — validate → record → optimistic rank →
        #       pessimistic confirm → assign (or cancel + reject).
        rider = self._riders.get(rider_id)
        if rider is None:
            raise UnknownRiderError(f"unknown rider: {rider_id}")
        self._validate_locations(pickup, drop)

        ride = Ride(id=self._make_id(), rider=rider, pickup=pickup, drop=drop)
        self._rides[ride.id] = ride  # orchestrator owns the truth from the start

        # OPTIMISTIC pick: read the available drivers and rank them with NO lock.
        candidates = self._strategy.rank(self._available_drivers(), pickup)
        # PESSIMISTIC confirm: re-check each candidate UNDER its lock; take the
        # first that is still AVAILABLE.
        chosen = self._confirm_candidate(candidates)

        if chosen is None:
            # Every request reaches a defined outcome: record it CANCELLED, then
            # reject. The ride row survives for audit instead of vanishing.
            ride.cancel()
            raise NoDriverAvailableError(
                f"no available driver for rider {rider_id}"
            )

        ride.assign(chosen)  # mutate the ride OUTSIDE the driver lock — it is private
        return ride

    def start_trip(self, ride_id: str) -> None:
        # role: move an assigned ride (and its driver) into the ON_TRIP phase.
        ride = self._require_ride(ride_id)
        ride.start()  # ASSIGNED → ONGOING; raises before we ever touch the driver
        driver = ride.driver
        assert driver is not None  # an ASSIGNED ride always has a driver
        with self._lock(driver):
            driver.start_trip()  # ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP

    def complete_ride(self, ride_id: str) -> None:
        # role: finish the trip and hand the driver back to the AVAILABLE pool.
        ride = self._require_ride(ride_id)
        ride.complete()  # ONGOING → COMPLETED; raises before we touch the driver
        driver = ride.driver
        assert driver is not None
        with self._lock(driver):
            driver.complete()  # ON_TRIP → AVAILABLE — the resource returns

    def set_driver_online(self, driver_id: str) -> None:
        # role: a driver starts accepting rides.
        driver = self._require_driver(driver_id)
        with self._lock(driver):
            driver.go_online()

    def set_driver_offline(self, driver_id: str) -> None:
        # role: a driver stops accepting rides (rejected mid-trip by the guard).
        driver = self._require_driver(driver_id)
        with self._lock(driver):
            driver.go_offline()

    # ── private helpers ──
    def _confirm_candidate(self, candidates: list[Driver]) -> Driver | None:
        # WHY: this is the heart of the design. The rank above was optimistic
        #      (no lock). Here we confirm pessimistically: for each candidate,
        #      take its lock and RE-CHECK that it is still AVAILABLE. If a rival
        #      rider claimed it between the rank and now, the re-check fails and
        #      we fall through to the next candidate. The first survivor is
        #      assigned atomically. Exhaust the list → None → no driver.
        for driver in candidates:
            with self._lock(driver):
                if driver.state == DriverState.AVAILABLE:
                    driver.assign()
                    return driver
        return None

    def _available_drivers(self) -> list[Driver]:
        return [d for d in self._drivers.values() if d.state == DriverState.AVAILABLE]

    def _validate_locations(self, *locations: Location) -> None:
        for loc in locations:
            if not loc.is_valid():
                raise InvalidLocationError(f"location out of range: {loc}")

    def _require_ride(self, ride_id: str) -> Ride:
        ride = self._rides.get(ride_id)
        if ride is None:
            raise UnknownRideError(f"unknown ride: {ride_id}")
        return ride

    def _require_driver(self, driver_id: str) -> Driver:
        driver = self._drivers.get(driver_id)
        if driver is None:
            raise UnknownDriverError(f"unknown driver: {driver_id}")
        return driver

    def _make_id(self) -> str:
        return f"ride-{next(self._ids)}"

    # ── read-only views (used by demos / tests / dashboards) ──
    def get_ride(self, ride_id: str) -> Ride:
        return self._require_ride(ride_id)

    def available_count(self) -> int:
        return len(self._available_drivers())


class ThreadSafeDispatchService(DispatchService):
    # ── WHY THIS CLASS ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    # The only thing that changes for concurrency. It keeps one lock per driver
    # in a dict, plus a small map lock that guards the dict itself. Everything
    # else — the matching loop, the API, the entities — is inherited unchanged.
    def __init__(
        self,
        strategy: DriverMatchStrategy,
        drivers: list[Driver] | None = None,
        riders: list[Rider] | None = None,
    ) -> None:
        super().__init__(strategy, drivers, riders)
        self._map_lock = threading.Lock()  # protects the dict of per-driver locks
        self._driver_locks: dict[str, threading.RLock] = {}

    @contextmanager
    def _lock(self, driver: Driver) -> Iterator[None]:
        # WHY: lock the MAP first, grab (or create) this driver's lock, release
        #      the map, THEN hold the driver lock around the critical section.
        #      Two threads creating the same key at once could corrupt the dict
        #      without the map lock. RLock (not Lock) so the same thread may
        #      re-enter if a guarded method calls another guarded method.
        with self._map_lock:
            lock = self._driver_locks.setdefault(driver.id, threading.RLock())
        with lock:
            yield

# Python 3.11+
"""Narrated demo driver for the Ride Sharing / Driver Dispatch system.

Run: `python3 main.py`. Each case prints a one-line "Case N ... -> OK".
"""

import threading

from models import (
    Driver,
    DriverState,
    IllegalRideTransitionError,
    Location,
    NoDriverAvailableError,
    Ride,
    Rider,
    RideState,
)
from ride_service import DispatchService, ThreadSafeDispatchService
from strategies import HighestRatedStrategy, NearestDriverStrategy


def _city(drivers: list[Driver], riders: list[Rider], strategy=None):
    strat = strategy or NearestDriverStrategy()
    return DispatchService(strat, drivers=drivers, riders=riders)


def main() -> None:
    # ── Case 1: request_ride matches the NEAREST available driver ──
    drivers = [
        Driver(id="d-far", location=Location(0.0, 10.0), rating=4.9),
        Driver(id="d-near", location=Location(0.0, 1.0), rating=4.2),
    ]
    riders = [Rider(id="r1", location=Location(0.0, 0.0))]
    svc = _city(drivers, riders)
    svc.set_driver_online("d-far")
    svc.set_driver_online("d-near")
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(5.0, 5.0))
    print(f"Case 1: request_ride -> {ride.id} assigned to {ride.driver.id} (state {ride.state.name})")
    assert ride.driver.id == "d-near"          # nearest wins
    assert ride.state == RideState.ASSIGNED
    assert ride.driver.state == DriverState.ASSIGNED
    print("Case 1: nearest available driver is matched and locked in -> OK")

    # ── Case 2: full trip lifecycle REQUESTED -> ASSIGNED -> ONGOING -> COMPLETED ──
    svc.start_trip(ride.id)
    assert ride.state == RideState.ONGOING and ride.driver.state == DriverState.ON_TRIP
    svc.complete_ride(ride.id)
    print(f"Case 2: lifecycle -> ride {ride.state.name}, driver {ride.driver.state.name}")
    assert ride.state == RideState.COMPLETED
    assert ride.driver.state == DriverState.AVAILABLE   # driver freed back to the pool
    print("Case 2: trip lifecycle drives both state machines in lock-step -> OK")

    # ── Case 3: the matching policy is pluggable (highest-rated wins) ──
    drivers = [
        Driver(id="d-near", location=Location(0.0, 1.0), rating=4.2),
        Driver(id="d-star", location=Location(0.0, 9.0), rating=5.0),
    ]
    riders = [Rider(id="r1", location=Location(0.0, 0.0))]
    svc = _city(drivers, riders, strategy=HighestRatedStrategy())
    svc.set_driver_online("d-near")
    svc.set_driver_online("d-star")
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    print(f"Case 3: HighestRatedStrategy -> picked {ride.driver.id} (rating {ride.driver.rating})")
    assert ride.driver.id == "d-star"          # rating beats distance under this policy
    print("Case 3: swapping the strategy changes the pick, not the service -> OK")

    # ── Case 4: no available driver -> typed rejection, ride recorded CANCELLED ──
    drivers = [Driver(id="d1", location=Location(0.0, 1.0), rating=4.5)]
    riders = [Rider(id="r1", location=Location(0.0, 0.0))]
    svc = _city(drivers, riders)
    # d1 is left OFFLINE on purpose — nobody is matchable.
    try:
        svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))
        assert False, "should have raised NoDriverAvailableError"
    except NoDriverAvailableError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: no online driver -> {e}")
    # the rejected request still left an auditable CANCELLED ride
    last = svc.get_ride("ride-1")
    assert last.state == RideState.CANCELLED
    # an illegal lifecycle jump is rejected too
    try:
        svc.start_trip("ride-1")               # cannot start a CANCELLED ride
        assert False, "should have raised IllegalRideTransitionError"
    except IllegalRideTransitionError as e:
        print(f"Case 4: illegal transition guarded -> {e}")
    print("Case 4: no-driver rejects with a typed error; illegal jumps are blocked -> OK")

    # ── Case 5: N riders race for K drivers — exactly K unique assignments ──
    k, n = 5, 30
    drivers = [Driver(id=f"d{i}", location=Location(0.0, float(i)), rating=4.5) for i in range(k)]
    riders = [Rider(id=f"r{i}", location=Location(0.0, 0.0)) for i in range(n)]
    ts = ThreadSafeDispatchService(NearestDriverStrategy(), drivers=drivers, riders=riders)
    for d in drivers:
        ts.set_driver_online(d.id)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
    wins: list[Ride] = []
    losses: list[NoDriverAvailableError] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(rider_id: str) -> None:
        barrier.wait()                          # all n hit request_ride at one instant
        try:
            r = ts.request_ride(rider_id, Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))
            with guard:
                wins.append(r)
        except NoDriverAvailableError as e:
            with guard:
                losses.append(e)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"r{i}",)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    unique_drivers = {r.driver.id for r in wins}
    print(f"Case 5: {n} riders, {k} drivers -> {len(wins)} wins on {len(unique_drivers)} unique drivers, {len(losses)} rejected")
    assert len(wins) == k
    assert len(unique_drivers) == k             # no two riders share a driver
    assert len(losses) == n - k
    assert sum(1 for d in drivers if d.state == DriverState.ASSIGNED) == k
    print("Case 5: under a real race, each driver is assigned exactly once -> OK")

    print("\nAll cases passed.")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

import pathlib
import sys

# Put the workspace root on sys.path so `from models import ...` works in tests.
sys.path.insert(0, str(pathlib.Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent))

# Python 3.11+
"""pytest suite for the Ride Sharing / Driver Dispatch system.

Covers: a happy path per public method, one test per typed exception, a boundary
test, an illegal-transition test, and the STRICT Barrier concurrency proof on
ThreadSafeDispatchService (N riders race for K drivers).
"""

import threading

import pytest

from models import (
    Driver,
    DriverState,
    IllegalDriverTransitionError,
    IllegalRideTransitionError,
    InvalidLocationError,
    Location,
    NoDriverAvailableError,
    Ride,
    Rider,
    RideState,
    UnknownDriverError,
    UnknownRideError,
    UnknownRiderError,
)
from ride_service import DispatchService, ThreadSafeDispatchService
from strategies import HighestRatedStrategy, NearestDriverStrategy


def make_service(strategy=None, n_drivers=2, online=True):
    strat = strategy or NearestDriverStrategy()
    drivers = [
        Driver(id=f"d{i}", location=Location(0.0, float(i + 1)), rating=4.0 + i * 0.1)
        for i in range(n_drivers)
    ]
    riders = [Rider(id="r1", location=Location(0.0, 0.0))]
    svc = DispatchService(strat, drivers=drivers, riders=riders)
    if online:
        for d in drivers:
            svc.set_driver_online(d.id)
    return svc, drivers, riders


# ── Location value object ──
def test_location_distance_is_symmetric_and_zero_to_self():
    a = Location(0.0, 0.0)
    b = Location(3.0, 4.0)
    assert a.distance_to(b) == pytest.approx(5.0)   # 3-4-5 triangle
    assert a.distance_to(b) == b.distance_to(a)
    assert a.distance_to(a) == 0.0


def test_location_validity_bounds():
    assert Location(90.0, 180.0).is_valid()
    assert not Location(91.0, 0.0).is_valid()
    assert not Location(0.0, 181.0).is_valid()


# ── happy path per public method ──
def test_request_ride_assigns_nearest_driver():
    svc, drivers, _ = make_service()
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    assert ride.state == RideState.ASSIGNED
    assert ride.driver.id == "d0"               # nearest (lng=1) beats d1 (lng=2)
    assert ride.driver.state == DriverState.ASSIGNED


def test_strategy_is_pluggable_highest_rated():
    svc, drivers, _ = make_service(strategy=HighestRatedStrategy())
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    assert ride.driver.id == "d1"               # higher rating wins over nearer


def test_full_trip_lifecycle():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    svc.start_trip(ride.id)
    assert ride.state == RideState.ONGOING
    assert ride.driver.state == DriverState.ON_TRIP
    svc.complete_ride(ride.id)
    assert ride.state == RideState.COMPLETED
    assert ride.driver.state == DriverState.AVAILABLE   # driver freed


def test_set_driver_offline_then_online():
    svc, drivers, _ = make_service()
    svc.set_driver_offline("d0")
    assert drivers[0].state == DriverState.OFFLINE
    assert svc.available_count() == 1
    svc.set_driver_online("d0")
    assert drivers[0].state == DriverState.AVAILABLE
    assert svc.available_count() == 2


def test_go_online_is_idempotent():
    svc, drivers, _ = make_service()
    svc.set_driver_online("d0")                 # already AVAILABLE — no-op, no raise
    assert drivers[0].state == DriverState.AVAILABLE


# ── one test per typed exception ──
def test_unknown_rider_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRiderError):
        svc.request_ride("ghost", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))


def test_invalid_location_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    with pytest.raises(InvalidLocationError):
        svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(999.0, 0.0))


def test_no_driver_available_raises_and_records_cancelled():
    svc, _, _ = make_service(online=False)      # all drivers OFFLINE
    with pytest.raises(NoDriverAvailableError):
        svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))
    assert svc.get_ride("ride-1").state == RideState.CANCELLED


def test_unknown_ride_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownRideError):
        svc.start_trip("ride-404")


def test_unknown_driver_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    with pytest.raises(UnknownDriverError):
        svc.set_driver_online("nobody")


def test_illegal_ride_transition_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    # ASSIGNED -> complete is illegal (must go through ONGOING first)
    with pytest.raises(IllegalRideTransitionError):
        svc.complete_ride(ride.id)


def test_illegal_driver_transition_offline_mid_trip_raises():
    svc, _, _ = make_service()
    ride = svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(2.0, 2.0))
    svc.start_trip(ride.id)                     # driver now ON_TRIP
    with pytest.raises(IllegalDriverTransitionError):
        svc.set_driver_offline(ride.driver.id)  # cannot vanish mid-trip


# ── boundary test ──
def test_single_driver_exhausted_after_one_assignment():
    svc, _, _ = make_service(n_drivers=1)
    svc.request_ride("r1", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))   # takes the only driver
    riders_extra = Rider(id="r2", location=Location(0.0, 0.0))
    svc._riders[riders_extra.id] = riders_extra
    with pytest.raises(NoDriverAvailableError):
        svc.request_ride("r2", Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))


# ── STRICT concurrency proof: N riders race for K drivers ──
def test_concurrent_requests_assign_each_driver_exactly_once():
    k, n = 8, 40
    drivers = [
        Driver(id=f"d{i}", location=Location(0.0, float(i)), rating=4.5)
        for i in range(k)
    ]
    riders = [Rider(id=f"r{i}", location=Location(0.0, 0.0)) for i in range(n)]
    svc = ThreadSafeDispatchService(NearestDriverStrategy(), drivers=drivers, riders=riders)
    for d in drivers:
        svc.set_driver_online(d.id)

    barrier = threading.Barrier(n)              # all n hit the method at one instant
    wins: list[Ride] = []
    losses: list[NoDriverAvailableError] = []
    guard = threading.Lock()

    def attempt(rider_id: str) -> None:
        barrier.wait()
        try:
            ride = svc.request_ride(rider_id, Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))
            with guard:
                wins.append(ride)
        except NoDriverAvailableError as exc:
            with guard:
                losses.append(exc)

    threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"r{i}",)) for i in range(n)]
    for t in threads:
        t.start()
    for t in threads:
        t.join()

    # exactly K succeed
    assert len(wins) == k
    # each winning ride sits on a UNIQUE driver — no two riders share one driver
    assert len({ride.driver.id for ride in wins}) == k
    # every win is a fully ASSIGNED ride
    assert all(ride.state == RideState.ASSIGNED for ride in wins)
    # the N-K losers all got the typed no-driver error
    assert len(losses) == n - k
    # and exactly K drivers actually ended up ASSIGNED (counted via entity state)
    assert sum(1 for d in drivers if d.state == DriverState.ASSIGNED) == k


def test_concurrent_requests_are_consistent_under_repeated_runs():
    # run the race a few times to shake out timing-dependent bugs
    for _ in range(5):
        k, n = 4, 20
        drivers = [Driver(id=f"d{i}", location=Location(0.0, float(i)), rating=4.5) for i in range(k)]
        riders = [Rider(id=f"r{i}", location=Location(0.0, 0.0)) for i in range(n)]
        svc = ThreadSafeDispatchService(NearestDriverStrategy(), drivers=drivers, riders=riders)
        for d in drivers:
            svc.set_driver_online(d.id)

        barrier = threading.Barrier(n)
        wins: list[Ride] = []
        guard = threading.Lock()

        def attempt(rider_id: str) -> None:
            barrier.wait()
            try:
                ride = svc.request_ride(rider_id, Location(0.0, 0.0), Location(1.0, 1.0))
                with guard:
                    wins.append(ride)
            except NoDriverAvailableError:
                pass

        threads = [threading.Thread(target=attempt, args=(f"r{i}",)) for i in range(n)]
        for t in threads:
            t.start()
        for t in threads:
            t.join()

        assert len(wins) == k
        assert len({ride.driver.id for ride in wins}) == k

All tests pass. Here's the complete, interview-ready design.

## ⏱️ 60-Minute Interview Plan

| Time | What you do | Mode |
|------|-------------|------|
| 0–5 min | **Say §1-2 out loud** — restate "match riders to nearby drivers", ask clarifying questions, propose scope cuts. Do not write yet. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 5–8 min | **Pin the API surface (§3.5)** — after listing entities, state the 5 public methods (`request_ride`, `start_trip`, `complete_ride`, `set_driver_online/offline`). "Before I design classes, here's the contract external code calls." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 8–14 min | **Draw the core class diagram** — only the 6 boxes that matter: `DispatchService`, `Driver`, `Ride`, `DriverMatchStrategy` (+2 concretes), `Location`. Mention `Rider` verbally as a data holder. | ✍️ DRAW |
| 14–16 min | **State patterns + concurrency flag** — name Strategy + State + Template Method, then immediately flag: "I see a check-then-act race in `request_ride` — two riders grab the same driver. I'll fix it with optimistic-pick / pessimistic-confirm at the end." | 🗣️ SAY |
| 16–18 min | **Walk §5.5 approach** — state the key insight (rank without a lock, confirm under a lock), explain the matching loop in plain words. | 🗣️ SAY |
| 18–38 min | **Write code in interview order** (see §6.0): enums → `Driver` + `Ride` state machines → `DriverMatchStrategy` → `DispatchService` skeleton → `request_ride()` → `start_trip` / `complete_ride`. | ✍️ WRITE |
| 38–45 min | **Demo Case 1 + Case 4** — happy assignment + no-driver rejection. If it's a coding env (Uber-style), run `main.py` here. | 🗣️ SAY / run |
| 45–55 min | **Concurrency answer** — describe optimistic-pick / pessimistic-confirm + the per-driver `RLock` hook. Write the thread-safe subclass ONLY if asked. | 🗣️ SAY (❓ code if asked) |
| 55–60 min | **Follow-up questions** — use the hardest follow-ups from §9 (distributed dispatch, 10M drivers, surge). | ❓ IF ASKED |

**Two interview modes — pick one at the top:**
- **Mode A — Whiteboard / no-run (Amazon, Google):** Skip §7 run output. Demo the assignment + race verbally. Write code but do not execute.
- **Mode B — Coding environment (Uber, Meta, startups):** Write and RUN `main.py` in §7. Show actual terminal output. Cases proved by the program.

**The one thing that makes interviewers flag you "strong hire":** Proactively say "I see a race condition — two riders racing for the last driver" right after the diagram, and name the fix as *optimistic-pick / pessimistic-confirm* before being asked. That single sentence signals you have built real dispatch systems.

## 1. Understanding the Problem & Clarifying Questions

### 1.1 What we're building (plain words)

We're building the **dispatch core** of something like Uber or Lyft — the piece that, the moment a rider taps "request", finds a good nearby driver and locks that driver to the trip. A rider gives us a pickup and a drop; we pick one *available* driver, hand the trip to them, and then track the ride as it goes from requested → assigned → on its way → done. The hard part isn't picking a driver once — it's that **many riders are tapping "request" at the same time and competing for the same handful of nearby drivers**, and we must never promise one driver to two riders.

### 1.2 Clarifying questions (candidate-led proposals)

**You:** "Let me scope this to the *dispatch* core — matching a rider to a driver and tracking the trip lifecycle. I'll treat the actual GPS streaming, maps/routing, fare calculation, and payments as separate systems out of scope for this round. Sound right?"
**Interviewer:** "Yep, that's exactly the focus."
*✓ scoped: dispatch + lifecycle only; routing/fares/payments are upstream/downstream systems.*

**You:** "For matching, I'll assume the *policy* for which driver to pick should be pluggable — nearest by default, but the business will want 'highest-rated' or 'cheapest' later. I'll build it as a strategy. OK?"
**Interviewer:** "Good, pluggable matching is what we want."
*✓ this is the Strategy seam — locks in §5.*

**You:** "When a rider requests and there's literally no available driver, I'll reject immediately with a typed error — no queueing or retry in v1. Queuing is a fair follow-up but I'd add it in §9. Agreed?"
**Interviewer:** "Agreed — reject is fine for v1."
*✓ no-driver = typed rejection; queueing flagged for extensibility.*

**You:** "I'll model both the driver and the ride as explicit **state machines** — a driver goes AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP → AVAILABLE, a ride goes REQUESTED → ASSIGNED → ONGOING → COMPLETED, with CANCELLED as the off-ramp. Illegal jumps raise. That OK as the backbone?"
**Interviewer:** "Yes — I specifically want to see the lifecycle modeled."
*✓ two coupled state machines; the entity guards its own transitions.*

**You:** "The interesting part is concurrency: many riders racing for scarce drivers. My plan is an **optimistic pick** — rank candidates with no lock — then a **pessimistic confirm** — re-check and claim the chosen driver under that driver's lock, falling through to the next candidate if I lost the race. Does going deep on that match what you want?"
**Interviewer:** "That's the heart of it, yes — I'll want the thread-safety story."
*✓ optimistic-pick / pessimistic-confirm is the centerpiece — §5.5-A.*

**You:** "Drivers and riders are pre-registered with the system; `request_ride` takes a `rider_id` and I reject unknown ids. New drivers joining the fleet at runtime I'll treat as a trivial registration call, out of the core flow. Fine?"
**Interviewer:** "Fine."
*✓ known drivers/riders; runtime fleet registration is a one-liner extension.*

### 1.3 Final Requirements (the whiteboard summary)

**Functional Requirements**

1. **Request a ride** — a rider requests with a pickup and drop; the system picks a suitable AVAILABLE driver and assigns the ride.
   - Returns the assigned `Ride` (with its driver) on success.
   - Rejects with `NoDriverAvailableError` if no driver can be confirmed, `InvalidLocationError` for bad coordinates, `UnknownRiderError` for an unknown rider.
2. **Pluggable matching policy** — which driver to pick is a strategy (nearest, highest-rated), swappable without touching the service.
3. **Trip lifecycle** — a ride moves REQUESTED → ASSIGNED → ONGOING → COMPLETED (or CANCELLED); a driver moves AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP → AVAILABLE.
   - `start_trip` and `complete_ride` advance both machines in lock-step; illegal jumps raise `IllegalRideTransitionError`.
4. **Driver availability** — drivers go online/offline; only AVAILABLE drivers are matchable.
   - Going offline mid-trip is rejected with `IllegalDriverTransitionError`.
5. **No double-assignment** — a driver is never promised to two riders at once, even under a concurrent burst of requests.
6. **Typed error handling** — every failure mode (no driver, bad location, unknown rider/driver/ride, illegal transition) raises a specific subclass of one `RideError` base.

**Out of scope**
- GPS / location streaming and map routing (upstream — we take a `Location` as given).
- Fare calculation, pricing, surge, payments (downstream — we just match and track).
- Notifications / push to the rider's phone (downstream — we return the `Ride`).
- Queueing / retry when no driver is free (flagged as a §9 extension — v1 rejects).

> 💬 **What you say to open:** "OK — before I model anything, the one thing I want to lock down is the concurrency contract: many riders compete for scarce drivers, and I must never double-assign. My plan is optimistic-pick then pessimistic-confirm. With that pinned, let me walk through the actors and flows."

## 2. Actors & Core Flows

**Actors**
- **Rider** — taps "request" with a pickup and drop; receives an assigned ride or a rejection.
- **Driver** — goes online/offline; gets assigned to a ride and drives it through its lifecycle.
- **Dispatch service** — the system itself: the brain that matches requests to drivers and tracks both state machines.

**Core flows**
1. Rider: `request_ride(pickup, drop)` → service ranks available drivers → confirms one under its lock → returns `Ride(ASSIGNED)`.
2. Trip: `start_trip(ride_id)` → ride ONGOING, driver ON_TRIP → `complete_ride(ride_id)` → ride COMPLETED, driver back to AVAILABLE.
3. Driver availability: `set_driver_online(id)` → AVAILABLE (matchable); `set_driver_offline(id)` → OFFLINE (skipped by matching).
4. No-driver: `request_ride` finds nobody AVAILABLE → ride recorded CANCELLED → `NoDriverAvailableError`.

## 3. Core Entities (accept / reject)

### 3.1 Candidate noun walkthrough

> **Ride** — A request that becomes a trip, with its own lifecycle (REQUESTED → ASSIGNED → ONGOING → COMPLETED/CANCELLED) and a reference to the driver serving it. It owns state AND transition behavior. *Accepted — it's a state machine.*
>
> **Driver** — A real person with a location, a rating, and an availability state machine (AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP). It is the scarce resource riders compete for, and only it may change its own `state`. *Accepted — the contended state machine.*
>
> **DispatchService** — The orchestrator that owns the registries and runs the matching loop. Not a "noun" from the prompt, but the coordinator every operation flows through. *Accepted — the brain.*
>
> **Location** — A pickup/drop coordinate. It has no lifecycle but it does have behavior worth owning: "how far to another point?" and "am I a valid coordinate?". A small, immutable value object. *Accepted — a frozen value object, not a bare tuple.*
>
> **Rider** — The person requesting. They mostly *call* the system, which leans "actor". But we DO store them so we can validate a `rider_id` and reject unknown callers, so it earns a thin entity. *Accepted — a lightweight, looked-up entity (no behavior).*
>
> **DriverMatchStrategy** — "Which driver to pick" is a pure policy decision the business will want to swap. That's a behavior, not data — it becomes a strategy interface, not an entity. *Accepted as a strategy interface (covered in §4.1), not a data entity.*
>
> **Trip** — Sounds like a separate thing, but a "trip" is just a `Ride` in its ONGOING phase — same object, different state. Making it its own class would split one lifecycle across two types. *Rejected — it's a state of `Ride`, not a new entity.*
>
> **Rating / DriverState / RideState** — A rating is a `float` field on `Driver`; the states are finite value sets. *Rejected as entities — `rating` is a field, the states are enums.*
>
> **Match / Assignment** — Tempting to model the "match" as an object. But the assignment IS the `Ride` holding a `driver` reference — a separate `Assignment` object would duplicate that link. *Rejected — the assignment lives as `Ride.driver`.*

### 3.2 Final entity table

| Entity | Responsibility | Key fields (typed) | Invariant it guards |
|---|---|---|---|
| `DispatchService` | Coordinate matching + lifecycle; own the registries | `_strategy: DriverMatchStrategy`, `_drivers: dict[str, Driver]`, `_riders: dict[str, Rider]`, `_rides: dict[str, Ride]` | Never sets a status directly; never double-assigns a driver |
| `Driver` | Be the matchable resource + guard its availability machine | `id: str` *(id)*, `location: Location`, `rating: float`, `state: DriverState` | Only AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP → AVAILABLE; illegal jumps raise |
| `Ride` | Track one request's trip lifecycle | `id: str` *(id)*, `rider: Rider`, `pickup/drop: Location`, `driver: Driver \| None` *(who serves it)*, `state: RideState` | REQUESTED → ASSIGNED → ONGOING → COMPLETED, or → CANCELLED; assigned exactly once |
| `Rider` | Be a looked-up requester | `id: str` *(id)*, `location: Location` | — (pure data holder) |
| `Location` | Be an immutable point + know distance/validity | `lat: float`, `lng: float` | Frozen — never mutated after creation |

**Enums**
- `DriverState` = OFFLINE, AVAILABLE, ASSIGNED, ON_TRIP
- `RideState` = REQUESTED, ASSIGNED, ONGOING, COMPLETED, CANCELLED

### 3.3 🎙️ Spoken intro

> 🎙️ "So the model has five objects plus a strategy. `DispatchService` is the brain — it owns the driver, rider, and ride registries and runs the matching loop, but it never sets a status field itself. `Driver` and `Ride` are the two interesting ones: each is a self-guarding state machine, so an illegal jump is impossible by construction, not by convention. `Location` is a frozen value object that knows how to measure distance to another point. `Rider` is deliberately thin — just an id and a location we look up. The pieces I left OUT are telling: there's no separate `Trip` class — a trip is just a `Ride` in its ONGOING state — and no `Assignment` class — the assignment is simply the `Ride` holding a `driver` reference. And 'which driver to pick' isn't an entity at all; it's a pluggable `DriverMatchStrategy`."

## 3.5 API / System Interface

Now that the entities exist, here is the **public surface** — the small set of methods external code (the rider app, the driver app) actually calls. Everything else is internal.

| Method | Signature | Returns | Raises | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `request_ride` | `(rider_id: str, pickup: Location, drop: Location) -> Ride` | the assigned `Ride` (REQUESTED → ASSIGNED) | `UnknownRiderError`, `InvalidLocationError`, `NoDriverAvailableError` | A rider taps "request"; system matches and assigns a driver |
| `start_trip` | `(ride_id: str) -> None` | — | `UnknownRideError`, `IllegalRideTransitionError` | The assigned driver picks the rider up; ride → ONGOING |
| `complete_ride` | `(ride_id: str) -> None` | — | `UnknownRideError`, `IllegalRideTransitionError` | Trip ends at the drop; ride → COMPLETED, driver freed |
| `set_driver_online` | `(driver_id: str) -> None` | — | `UnknownDriverError`, `IllegalDriverTransitionError` | A driver starts accepting rides → AVAILABLE |
| `set_driver_offline` | `(driver_id: str) -> None` | — | `UnknownDriverError`, `IllegalDriverTransitionError` | A driver stops accepting rides (rejected mid-trip) |

> 🎙️ "The whole system is five public calls: one that takes requests in (`request_ride`), two that drive the trip lifecycle (`start_trip`, `complete_ride`), and two availability toggles. Everything else is internal — the strategy ranks, the driver confirms itself under a lock, the entities guard their own transitions. If you handed me this interface cold, you'd know exactly what the dispatch core does without seeing a single class. Notice `request_ride` is the only one that returns a value — the rest are commands that mutate lifecycle state."

Every method here maps to a §1.3 requirement (1→`request_ride`, 3→`start_trip`/`complete_ride`, 4→`set_driver_online`/`set_driver_offline`), and the orchestrator has no public method that isn't in this table.

## 4. Class Design

### 4.1 Per-class derivation

#### 4.1.1 `DispatchService` — the orchestrator (first)

**(a) Role.** The system's public API and brain — external code calls it to request rides, drive the trip lifecycle, and toggle driver availability. Every operation flows through here, but it delegates *which driver* to a strategy and *state transitions* to the entities.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "picks a suitable AVAILABLE driver" | the registry of drivers, keyed by id |
| "request takes a `rider_id`, reject unknown" | the registry of riders, keyed by id |
| "track the trip lifecycle" / orchestrator owns the truth | the registry of rides, keyed by id |
| "matching is pluggable" | a reference to the chosen `DriverMatchStrategy` |
| numbered ride ids for readable demos/tests | an id counter |

```
class DispatchService:
    - strategy: DriverMatchStrategy
    - drivers: Map<str, Driver>
    - riders:  Map<str, Rider>
    - rides:   Map<str, Ride>
    - ids:     counter
```

**State placement trade-off.** A driver's *availability* (`state`) is **physical** state — it describes the driver's own condition (online? mid-trip?), so it lives on `Driver`. The *assignment link* (which ride a driver serves) is **relational** — it's a system-managed relationship — so it lives as `Ride.driver` in the orchestrator's ride registry, not as a back-pointer on `Driver`. One direction of the link only: rides know their driver; drivers don't carry a ride id. That keeps the driver a clean, reusable resource.

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "rider requests a ride" | `request_ride(rider_id, pickup, drop) -> Ride` |
| "driver picks rider up" | `start_trip(ride_id) -> None` |
| "trip ends" | `complete_ride(ride_id) -> None` |
| "driver goes online" | `set_driver_online(driver_id) -> None` |
| "driver goes offline" | `set_driver_offline(driver_id) -> None` |
| confirm a driver atomically (concurrency hook) | `_lock(driver)` context manager (no-op in core) |

```
class DispatchService:
    - strategy: DriverMatchStrategy
    - drivers:  Map<str, Driver>
    - riders:   Map<str, Rider>
    - rides:    Map<str, Ride>
    - ids:      counter

    + DispatchService(strategy, drivers, riders)
    + request_ride(rider_id, pickup, drop) -> Ride | error
    + start_trip(ride_id) -> void | error
    + complete_ride(ride_id) -> void | error
    + set_driver_online(driver_id) -> void | error
    + set_driver_offline(driver_id) -> void | error
    # _lock(driver) -> contextmanager      // no-op hook; subclass overrides
    - _confirm_candidate(candidates) -> Driver | None
    - _available_drivers() -> Driver[]
    - _validate_locations(*locations) -> void | error
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why does `request_ride` return the whole `Ride` but the others return `None`?** The rider needs the ride id + assigned driver back to track the trip; `start_trip`/`complete_ride` are commands whose "signal" is the state change, so returning nothing keeps them honest.
- **Why a `_lock` no-op hook here?** So the clean core has zero lock code. The thread-safe subclass overrides this one method — the concurrency diff is a single method, not a rewrite.
- **Why does the service never write `driver.state = ...` directly?** Information Expert: the driver owns its machine. The service asks (`driver.assign()`), so the *only* place a transition rule lives is the entity.

#### 4.1.2 `Driver` — the contended state machine

**(a) Role.** A driver is the scarce resource riders compete for. It guards its own availability state machine — only it may change `state`, and every transition is checked.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "only AVAILABLE drivers are matched" | its current `state` |
| "nearest / highest-rated matching" | its `location` and `rating` |
| identify it in the registry | its `id` |

```
class Driver:
    - id: str
    - location: Location
    - rating: float
    - state: DriverState = OFFLINE
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "drivers go online" | `go_online() -> void` |
| "drivers go offline (not mid-trip)" | `go_offline() -> void | error` |
| "claim this driver for a ride" | `assign() -> void | error` |
| "trip starts" | `start_trip() -> void | error` |
| "trip ends, driver freed" | `complete() -> void | error` |

```
class Driver:
    - id: str
    - location: Location
    - rating: float
    - state: DriverState = OFFLINE

    + Driver(id, location, rating)
    + go_online() -> void | error      // OFFLINE -> AVAILABLE (idempotent from AVAILABLE)
    + go_offline() -> void | error     // AVAILABLE -> OFFLINE (idempotent from OFFLINE)
    + assign() -> void | error         // AVAILABLE -> ASSIGNED
    + start_trip() -> void | error     // ASSIGNED -> ON_TRIP
    + complete() -> void | error       // ON_TRIP -> AVAILABLE
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why is `assign()` separate from the service picking the driver?** The pick (strategy) and the claim (transition) are different concerns. `assign()` is the *one line* the per-driver lock protects — the very next claimer sees ASSIGNED and is turned away.
- **Why is `go_online` idempotent but `assign` not?** Toggling online twice is harmless (a no-op); claiming an already-claimed driver is a real bug, so it raises.

#### 4.1.3 `Ride` — the lifecycle state machine

**(a) Role.** A ride tracks one request's journey. It is the *other* state machine, and unlike a driver it belongs to exactly one request, so it's never contended.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "track lifecycle REQUESTED → … → COMPLETED" | its `state` |
| "who requested it" | the `rider` |
| "from where to where" | `pickup`, `drop` |
| "which driver serves it" | the `driver` reference (None until assigned) |

```
class Ride:
    - id: str
    - rider: Rider
    - pickup: Location
    - drop: Location
    - driver: Driver | None = None
    - state: RideState = REQUESTED
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "assign a driver to the ride" | `assign(driver) -> void | error` |
| "driver picks rider up" | `start() -> void | error` |
| "trip ends" | `complete() -> void | error` |
| "no driver / called off" | `cancel() -> void | error` |

```
class Ride:
    - id: str
    - rider: Rider
    - pickup: Location
    - drop: Location
    - driver: Driver | None = None
    - state: RideState = REQUESTED

    + Ride(id, rider, pickup, drop)
    + assign(driver) -> void | error   // REQUESTED -> ASSIGNED, records driver
    + start() -> void | error          // ASSIGNED -> ONGOING
    + complete() -> void | error       // ONGOING -> COMPLETED
    + cancel() -> void | error         // REQUESTED/ASSIGNED -> CANCELLED
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why record the rejected request as CANCELLED instead of returning nothing?** Every request reaches a defined, auditable outcome — the ride row survives so ops can see "rider r1 found no driver at 5pm" instead of a silent drop.
- **Why does `assign(driver)` take the driver but `start()`/`complete()` don't?** The driver link is set once at assignment; afterwards the ride already knows its driver, so the lifecycle methods just advance state.

#### 4.1.4 `Rider` — the looked-up data holder

**(a) Role.** A thin entity the service stores so it can validate a `rider_id` and reject unknown callers. No behavior, no lifecycle.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "reject unknown rider id" | its `id` |
| "where the rider is" | its `location` |

```
class Rider:
    - id: str
    - location: Location
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| (none — pure data holder) | — |

```
class Rider:
    - id: str
    - location: Location

    + Rider(id, location)
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why is `Rider` an entity at all and not just an actor?** Borderline. It earns a thin class only because the orchestrator needs a registry to validate ids against; if we didn't validate, `Rider` would collapse into a parameter.

#### 4.1.5 `Location` — the value object

**(a) Role.** An immutable map point that knows how to measure distance to another point and whether it's a valid coordinate.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| "pickup / drop coordinates" | `lat`, `lng` |

```
class Location:   // frozen
    - lat: float
    - lng: float
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this class |
|---|---|
| "rank by distance to pickup" | `distance_to(other) -> float` |
| "reject invalid coordinates" | `is_valid() -> bool` |

```
class Location:   // frozen value object
    - lat: float
    - lng: float

    + Location(lat, lng)
    + distance_to(other) -> float    // flat-plane approximation
    + is_valid() -> bool             // lat in [-90,90], lng in [-180,180]
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why frozen?** A coordinate never changes — a rider who moves gets a NEW `Location`. Freezing makes accidental mutation a runtime error and lets us treat locations as safe-to-share values.
- **Why does `Location` own `distance_to`?** Information Expert — the object holding the coordinates is the one that should compute distance. The strategy just calls it; it never reaches into `lat`/`lng`.

#### 4.1.6 `DriverMatchStrategy` — the matching seam (interface)

**(a) Role.** The abstraction the service depends on for "which driver to prefer". It **ranks** available drivers into an ordered candidate list — it never claims one. That read-only contract is exactly what enables the lock-free optimistic pick.

**(b) State derivation.**

| Requirement | What this class must remember |
|---|---|
| (stateless policy) | — |

```
interface DriverMatchStrategy:
    (no state)
```

**(c) Operations derivation.**

| Need from requirements | Method on this interface |
|---|---|
| "pick nearest / highest-rated" | `rank(drivers, pickup) -> Driver[]` |

```
interface DriverMatchStrategy:
    + rank(drivers: Driver[], pickup: Location) -> Driver[]   // ordered, MUST NOT mutate

class NearestDriverStrategy implements DriverMatchStrategy:
    + rank(drivers, pickup) -> Driver[]    // sort by distance_to(pickup) asc

class HighestRatedStrategy implements DriverMatchStrategy:
    + rank(drivers, pickup) -> Driver[]    // sort by rating desc, distance asc tie-break
```

**(d) Design choices.**
- **Why return a *list*, not a single driver?** The single-driver design forces the strategy to know about locking and retries. By returning a ranked list, the service can fall through to the next candidate when its first pick was snatched — with zero help from the policy.
- **Why must `rank` not mutate?** The service calls it *outside* any lock (the optimistic read). A mutating strategy would make that read unsafe and defeat the whole pattern.

## 5. Design Patterns & Principles

### 5.1 Information Expert (the foundational principle)

> "I followed Information Expert: each class owns the state and behavior closest to the data it holds. `DispatchService` owns matching and the three registries because it's the only thing that sees *all* drivers and rides at once. `Driver` enforces its own availability transitions because that rule is intrinsic to a driver — nobody else should be allowed to flip it ASSIGNED. `Ride` owns its lifecycle for the same reason. `Location` owns `distance_to` because it's the only thing that holds the coordinates. The strategy owns *ranking* because 'which driver is best' is a policy, not data — and policy is exactly the thing that changes."

This is why the patterns below aren't buzzwords — they fall out of who-owns-what.

### 5.2 Applied patterns (each one earned)

**Strategy — `DriverMatchStrategy`**
- **Why it fits HERE:** "which driver to pick" is *exactly* the thing the business tweaks — nearest today, highest-rated tomorrow, surge-aware next quarter. Extracting `DriverMatchStrategy` means the service never hardcodes the policy, and — critically — the strategy is **read-only** (it ranks, it doesn't claim), which is what lets us pick without a lock.
- **Interface + concretes:** `DriverMatchStrategy.rank(drivers, pickup) -> list[Driver]` → `NearestDriverStrategy`, `HighestRatedStrategy`.
- 🎙️ "The service asks a strategy to *rank* the drivers; it never decides the policy itself. Nearest is just the default — highest-rated is one new class."
- 💬 **Layman:** "The dispatcher doesn't decide who's 'best' — it asks a rule-of-the-day. Swap the rule, the dispatcher doesn't change."

**State machine — `Driver` and `Ride`**
- **Why it fits HERE:** both have a finite, ordered set of legal states with illegal jumps that must be impossible. Modeling them as guarded machines means a six-months-later refactor can't accidentally mark a driver ON_TRIP while OFFLINE.
- **Realized as:** `Driver.assign/start_trip/complete` and `Ride.assign/start/complete/cancel`, each raising `Illegal*TransitionError` on a bad jump.
- 🎙️ "Each entity is the only class allowed to change its own status, and every transition is checked. The orchestrator coordinates two machines; it never reaches in."
- 💬 **Layman:** "A driver and a ride each have a little rulebook of 'what state can follow what'. They enforce their own rulebook."

**Template Method — `DispatchService._lock`**
- **Why it fits HERE:** the clean core must stay lock-free and readable, yet the thread-safe version must guard the confirm step. A no-op `_lock` hook that the subclass overrides keeps the entire concurrency story in one method.
- **Realized as:** `DispatchService._lock` yields (no-op); `ThreadSafeDispatchService._lock` wraps a per-driver `RLock`.
- 🎙️ "My core has zero lock imports. The thread-safe version overrides one placeholder method. The diff is about ten lines."
- 💬 **Layman:** "I left a labeled blank where a lock *would* go. The safe version fills in that one blank — nothing else moves."

### 5.3 SOLID (specific, tied to named classes)

- **Single Responsibility:** `Driver` owns availability, `Ride` owns trip lifecycle, `DriverMatchStrategy` owns selection, `DispatchService` owns coordination. Four reasons to change, four classes.
- **Open/Closed:** a new matching rule = a new `DriverMatchStrategy` subclass, zero edits to `DispatchService`.
- **Liskov:** `ThreadSafeDispatchService` is a drop-in for `DispatchService` — same public contract, only `_lock` differs. Every test that runs on the base runs on the subclass.
- **Dependency Inversion:** `DispatchService` depends on the `DriverMatchStrategy` abstraction, never on `NearestDriverStrategy` directly.

### 5.4 EARN YOUR PATTERNS

- **Strategy** is real: `DriverMatchStrategy` is an ABC with two concretes, injected into the service constructor — survives `Cmd-F`.
- **State** is real: the `Illegal*TransitionError` guards are in `models.py` and exercised by tests.
- **Template Method** is real: `_lock` is a no-op in the base and overridden in the subclass.
- **NOT claimed:** no Observer (no notifications in v1 — that's downstream, flagged out of scope), no Factory (we don't translate one type into another), no Singleton (the service is just instantiated once by the caller — that's a Facade-ish entry point, not an enforced Singleton).

## 5.5 Core Algorithms & Approach

### A. The Key Insight

> **Insight:** Split matching into a **lock-free optimistic pick** (rank the candidates) and a **locked pessimistic confirm** (claim the chosen driver under *that driver's* lock, falling through to the next candidate if it was taken). The strategy returns an ordered *list*, never a single driver — that list is what makes the fall-through possible.
>
> **Why it works:** Ranking is a pure read with no shared-state mutation, so it's safe to do without any lock and it's where the expensive sort lives. The only thing that's actually contended is the single line that flips a driver AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED. By shrinking the critical section to *just that flip per driver* — and re-checking availability inside the lock — two riders who both ranked the same driver first can't both claim it: the first wins, the second's re-check sees ASSIGNED and moves to candidate #2. Different drivers don't block each other at all.
>
> **What breaks without it:** The naive version holds one global lock around the whole `request_ride` (kills throughput — every rider in the whole city serializes through one mutex even when they want different drivers), OR it locks nothing and does `if driver.state == AVAILABLE: driver.state = ASSIGNED` (the classic check-then-act race — two riders both see AVAILABLE, both assign, one driver now owes two trips). The optimistic-pick / pessimistic-confirm split gives correctness *and* concurrency: the global sort is lock-free, and only same-driver contenders ever serialize, for microseconds.

### B. Per-Operation Algorithm

**`request_ride(rider_id, pickup, drop)` — the pipeline (race-prone method)**

1. Look up `rider_id`; if unknown → raise `UnknownRiderError`.
2. Validate `pickup` and `drop`; if either out of range → raise `InvalidLocationError`.
3. Create a `Ride` in REQUESTED state, store it in the ride registry (orchestrator owns the truth now).
4. **Optimistic pick (NO lock):** read the AVAILABLE drivers, ask `strategy.rank(...)` for an ordered candidate list.
5. **Pessimistic confirm:** for each candidate, take *its* lock; re-check `state == AVAILABLE`; if so, `driver.assign()` and stop; if not, try the next.
6. If no candidate confirmed → `ride.cancel()` (REQUESTED → CANCELLED), raise `NoDriverAvailableError`.
7. `ride.assign(chosen)` (outside the driver lock — the ride is private to this request); return the ride.

> 🎙️ **This method has the check-then-act race.** "Steps 4-5 are the heart. Step 4 is optimistic — I rank with *no* lock because ranking only reads. Step 5 is the critical section, and it's tiny: per candidate I take that one driver's lock and re-check AVAILABLE — that re-check is the whole game, because between my rank and my claim another rider may have grabbed this driver. If they did, my re-check fails and I fall through to candidate #2 — that's why step 4 returns a *list*. Step 7 mutates the ride *outside* the lock on purpose: the ride belongs only to this request, so nothing else can touch it; only the driver is shared."

**`start_trip(ride_id)` — lifecycle advance**

1. Look up `ride_id`; if unknown → raise `UnknownRideError`.
2. `ride.start()` (ASSIGNED → ONGOING); raises `IllegalRideTransitionError` if the ride isn't ASSIGNED — this runs *before* we touch the driver, so a bad ride never half-mutates a driver.
3. Under the driver's lock, `ride.driver.start_trip()` (ASSIGNED → ON_TRIP).

> 🎙️ "Lifecycle advance — two coupled machines. Step 2 advances the ride and is the guard; only if it passes do I advance the driver in step 3. Order matters: the ride's guard fires first, so an illegal call can never leave the driver in a half-changed state."

**`complete_ride(ride_id)` — lifecycle finish + free the resource**

1. Look up `ride_id`; if unknown → raise `UnknownRideError`.
2. `ride.complete()` (ONGOING → COMPLETED); raises if not ONGOING.
3. Under the driver's lock, `ride.driver.complete()` (ON_TRIP → AVAILABLE) — the driver returns to the matchable pool.

> 🎙️ "Same shape as start, but the payoff is step 3: completing frees the driver back to AVAILABLE, so they immediately re-enter the candidate pool for the next rider. The driver lock here matters because a concurrent `request_ride` could be confirming this very driver the instant it becomes available."

**`set_driver_online(driver_id)` / `set_driver_offline(driver_id)` — availability toggles**

1. Look up `driver_id`; if unknown → raise `UnknownDriverError`.
2. Under the driver's lock, `driver.go_online()` / `driver.go_offline()`; offline raises `IllegalDriverTransitionError` if the driver is ASSIGNED/ON_TRIP.

> 🎙️ "These are one guard and one transition. The lock is here too — *every* mutation of a driver's state goes through `_lock`, not just the confirm. Otherwise a driver could flip offline at the exact moment a rider is confirming them."

**DRY RUN — `request_ride("r1", pickup=(0,0), drop=(5,5))`** with drivers `d-near@(0,1) rating 4.2 AVAILABLE`, `d-far@(0,10) rating 4.9 AVAILABLE`, strategy = Nearest:

| Step | Action | State change |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | look up `r1` | found ✓ |
| 2 | validate (0,0),(5,5) | both valid ✓ |
| 3 | create `Ride` REQUESTED | `rides = { "ride-1": Ride(REQUESTED, driver=None) }` |
| 4 | `_available_drivers()` → `[d-near, d-far]`; `rank(..., (0,0))` | candidates = `[d-near (dist 1), d-far (dist 10)]` |
| 5 | lock `d-near`, re-check AVAILABLE ✓ → `d-near.assign()` | `d-near.state: AVAILABLE → ASSIGNED`; loop stops |
| 7 | `ride.assign(d-near)` (outside lock) | `ride-1.state: REQUESTED → ASSIGNED`, `ride-1.driver = d-near` |
| → | return `ride-1` | caller gets an ASSIGNED ride on `d-near` |

> *Edge probe: what if a second rider calls `request_ride` while step 5 is mid-flight for `d-near`?* *Both ranked `d-near` first. The lock serializes them: whoever takes `d-near`'s lock first flips it ASSIGNED; the second thread's re-check inside the lock sees ASSIGNED, skips it, and confirms `d-far` instead. No double-assign. Without the lock + re-check, both would assign `d-near`.*

### C. Data Structure Choices

| Data Structure | Used For | Why THIS (vs alternative considered) | Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|
| `dict[str, Driver]` | driver registry | O(1) lookup by id for online/offline + the available scan; a list would be O(n) to find a driver by id | O(1) get/set, O(n) scan for available |
| `dict[str, Ride]` | ride registry | `start_trip`/`complete_ride` look up by id in O(1); also the audit trail of CANCELLED rides | O(1) |
| `dict[str, Rider]` | rider registry | O(1) validation of `rider_id`; alternative (no registry) loses the unknown-rider check | O(1) |
| ranked `list[Driver]` from strategy | optimistic candidate order | a *list* (not a single pick) is what enables fall-through to the next candidate on a lost race | O(n log n) sort, n = available drivers |
| `dict[str, RLock]` + map lock | per-driver locks (thread-safe subclass) | one lock per driver so different drivers don't block each other; one global lock would serialize the whole city | O(1) lock fetch |

I deliberately use a **linear scan + sort** of available drivers rather than a spatial index (quadtree / geo-hash). At a single dispatch zone's scale (tens to low hundreds of online drivers) the sort is trivial. **I'd switch to a geo-spatial index (e.g. an H3/geohash bucket + k-nearest query) when a zone crosses a few thousand online drivers** — at that point the full scan per request becomes the hot spot.

### D. Tricky Logic — claim / proof / fix

> **🧠 Claim:** The availability re-check must happen *inside* the per-driver lock, not before it.
> **🔬 Proof:** Two riders both rank `d7` first. Rider A checks "AVAILABLE" outside the lock, then waits for the lock. Rider B does the same. A takes the lock, assigns, releases. B takes the lock — but B already "checked" AVAILABLE earlier, so if the re-check isn't *inside* the lock, B assigns `d7` too. Double-assign.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `_confirm_candidate` does `with self._lock(driver): if driver.state == AVAILABLE: driver.assign()`. The check and the act are one atomic step under the lock. The 40-thread test in §7/§9 exercises exactly this.

> **🧠 Claim:** Mutating the `Ride` outside the lock is safe; mutating the `Driver` is not.
> **🔬 Proof:** A driver is shared — many riders' candidate lists contain it. A ride is created fresh inside `request_ride` and referenced by no one else until it's returned. So the ride has exactly one writer (this thread); the driver has many.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `driver.assign()` is inside `_confirm_candidate`'s `with self._lock`; `ride.assign(chosen)` is the last line, outside the lock. Narrate this — it shows you know *what* needs guarding, not just "wrap everything".

> **🧠 Claim:** `start_trip` must advance the *ride* before the *driver*.
> **🔬 Proof:** If we flipped the driver ON_TRIP first and the ride's guard then rejected (ride wasn't ASSIGNED), the driver would be stuck ON_TRIP with no valid ride — a corrupt pair.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `ride.start()` runs first and raises on a bad state; only if it passes do we call `ride.driver.start_trip()`.

> **🧠 Claim:** `go_online` must be idempotent but `assign` must not.
> **🔬 Proof:** A driver app may send "online" twice on a flaky network — that should be harmless. But claiming an already-ASSIGNED driver is a genuine double-assign and must be loud.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `go_online` returns silently if already AVAILABLE; `assign` raises `IllegalDriverTransitionError` if not AVAILABLE.

> **🧠 Claim:** The distance metric only needs to be *order-correct*, not *true distance*.
> **🔬 Proof:** Matching only uses distance to *rank* candidates. A flat-plane (Euclidean) metric and true haversine agree on ordering for nearby points, so the cheaper metric gives the same pick.
> **🛠️ Fix in code:** `Location.distance_to` uses `math.hypot` and is documented as an approximation; swapping to haversine changes the method body only, never a call site.

### E. The "what I'd write WRONG first" anti-example

> **❌ Naive version a junior would write:**
> ```
> def request_ride(self, rider_id, pickup, drop):
>     driver = self.find_nearest_available(pickup)   # bare lookup, no lock
>     driver.state = DriverState.ASSIGNED             # direct write, check-then-act
>     return Ride(..., driver=driver, state=ASSIGNED)
> ```
> **Why it's wrong:** Two failure modes. (1) Direct status write bypasses the state machine, so illegal transitions become possible and the rule lives in N call sites instead of one. (2) `find` then `write` is check-then-act with no lock — two riders both find the same nearest driver and both assign it. The correct version asks a *strategy* for a ranked list, calls `driver.assign()` so the entity guards itself, and confirms the driver under a per-driver lock with a re-check, falling through to the next candidate on a lost race.
